questions.q:
a. What is the main objective of preparing a cash flow statement?
b. State the types of cash flow statements.
c. Explain the importance of cash flow statement in business.
questions.answer:
The main objective of preparing a cash flow statement is to present the inflows and outflows of cash and cash equivalents of an enterprise over a particular period of time.
There are three types of cash flow statements:
1. Direct method: This method presents the major inflows and outflows of cash and cash equivalents.
2. Indirect method: This method presents the cash flows from operating activities by adjusting net income for non-cash items.
3. Combination of direct and indirect methods: This method combines the direct and indirect methods to present the cash flows.
The importance of cash flow statement in business is that it helps in assessing the liquidity and financial flexibility of a business. It also helps in predicting the future cash flows and making informed decisions about investment and financing.
marking_scheme: Explain the main objective of cash flow statement (1 mark)
State the types of cash flow statements (2 marks)
Explain the importance of cash flow statement (2 marks)

questions.q: What are the essential characteristics of a true and fair view financial statement? Explain each characteristic with an example.
questions.answer:
The essential characteristics of a true and fair view financial statement are:
1. Accuracy: The financial statement should be free from any material misstatements.
Example: If a company has a large amount of accounts payable that are not recorded, the financial statement will not accurately reflect the company's liabilities.
2. Completeness: The financial statement should include all the necessary information to understand the company's financial position.
Example: If a company has a significant amount of assets that are not recorded, the financial statement will not provide a complete picture of the company's assets.
3. Neutrality: The financial statement should not be biased towards any particular group of stakeholders.
Example: If a company's management has a personal interest in a particular transaction and records it at an inflated price, the financial statement will not be neutral.
4. Comparability: The financial statement should be presented in a manner that allows for comparison with other companies.
Example: If a company presents its financial statement in a unique format that is not comparable with other companies, it will not meet the comparability requirement.
5. Understandability: The financial statement should be presented in a clear and concise manner that is easily understandable by the users.
marking_scheme: Explain the characteristic of accuracy (1 mark)
Explain the characteristic of completeness (1 mark)
Explain the characteristic of neutrality (1 mark)
Explain the characteristic of comparability (1 mark)
Explain the characteristic of understandability (1 mark)

questions.q:
a. Explain the concept of materiality in accounting.
b. State the factors that affect the materiality of an item.
c. Explain how materiality is applied in the preparation of financial statements.
questions.answer:
The concept of materiality in accounting refers to the significance or magnitude of an item in relation to the financial statements of an enterprise. An item is considered material if its omission or misstatement could influence the decisions of the users of the financial statements.
The factors that affect the materiality of an item are:
1. Size: The size of the item in relation to the total amounts presented in the financial statements.
2. Nature: The nature of the item, such as its relevance to the financial statements and its impact on the decisions of the users.
3. Location: The location of the item in the financial statements, such as its prominence or visibility.
Materiality is applied in the preparation of financial statements by considering the following:
1. The size of the item in relation to the total amounts presented in the financial statements.
2. The nature of the item, such as its relevance to the financial statements and its impact on the decisions of the users.
3. The location of the item in the financial statements, such as its prominence or visibility.
marking_scheme: Explain the concept of materiality (1 mark)
State the factors that affect materiality (2 marks)
Explain how materiality is applied (2 marks)

questions.q:
a. Explain the concept of going concern.
b. State the assumptions of going concern.
c. Explain how the going concern concept affects the preparation of financial statements.
questions.answer:
The concept of going concern refers to the assumption that an enterprise will continue to operate for the foreseeable future and will be able to realize its assets and discharge its liabilities in the normal course of business.
The assumptions of going concern are:
1. The enterprise has sufficient resources to meet its obligations.
2. The enterprise will continue to operate in the normal course of business.
3. The enterprise will be able to realize its assets and discharge its liabilities in the normal course of business.
The going concern concept affects the preparation of financial statements by:
1. Assuring the users that the enterprise will continue to operate and will be able to realize its assets and discharge its liabilities.
2. Allowing the enterprise to use the accrual method of accounting, which matches revenues with expenses.
3. Enabling the enterprise to recognize assets and liabilities at their carrying value.
marking_scheme: Explain the concept of going concern (1 mark)
State the assumptions of going concern (2 marks)
Explain how going concern affects financial statements (2 marks)

questions.q:
a. Explain the concept of prudence in accounting.
b. State the principles of prudence.
c. Explain how prudence is applied in the preparation of financial statements.
questions.answer:
The concept of prudence in accounting refers to the exercise of caution and skepticism in the preparation of financial statements. It involves taking a conservative approach to accounting decisions and being cautious in the recognition of revenue and assets.
The principles of prudence are:
1. Do not recognize revenue until it has been earned.
2. Do not recognize assets until they have been realized.
3. Do not delay the recognition of liabilities until it is certain that the liability will not arise.
Prudence is applied in the preparation of financial statements by:
1. Recognizing revenue and assets only when they have been earned and realized.
2. Delaying the recognition of liabilities until it is certain that the liability will arise.
3. Taking a conservative approach to accounting decisions.
marking_scheme: Explain the concept of prudence (1 mark)
State the principles of prudence (2 marks)
Explain how prudence is applied (2 marks)
